title: He Raised $26M Betting on Probabilities, Not Guesses | Koah Labs, Nic Baird
summary: Nic Baird, founder of Koah Labs, argues that ad-based monetization is the future for AI tools rather than subscriptions. He contends that the real bottleneck for AI agents is not technology but the time required to build human trust in these systems.
writeup: Nick Barrett, CEO of KA Labs, outlines how probabilistic reasoning under uncertainty applies to startup decisions, career bets, and sailing tactics. KA Labs builds an ad monetization platform for AI tools (described as "AdSense for AI"), which has raised $26 million and now reaches single-digit millions of users. The through-line is that high-variance, low-floor bets often carry better expected value than certain but capped outcomes, and that the market consensus on AI subscriptions displacing ads was a contrarian signal Barrett chose to bet against.
key_points: KA Labs has raised $26 million and is scaling an ad monetization layer for AI apps, analogous to AdSense, premised on the observation that subscription-only monetization leaves most AI developers burning inference costs with no revenue path.

Barrett's core decision framework treats every career or business choice as a probability-weighted expected value: a 1% chance at $1 trillion yields an expected $10 billion, outperforming a certain $1 million, so low-probability, high-upside bets can be rationally superior.

The contrarian thesis on AI advertising crystallized at TechCrunch Disrupt 2024, where consumer VCs were publicly declaring ads dead and subscription dominant; Barrett treated that market consensus as a signal to investigate rather than follow.

Sailing provides Barrett's operating analogy for decision-making under uncertainty: positioning toward a predicted wind shift is correct even when the rest of the fleet goes the other way, but the size of the bet should scale with confidence and the cost of being wrong, not with instinct alone.

South Park Commons was the inflection point where Barrett moved from self-doubt to building: exposure to fast builders (his future co-founders Mike and Heric, who shipped a new project every month) gave him evidence that he was capable of building, not just a belief.
Reducing the friction to build, through tooling and community, is as important as finding motivating factors such as engaged early customers who pull ideas out of you by asking for specific things.
